# A-Src-13: Video #09 — Most Essential Tahreeb (Re-extraction)

**Source:** `C:\CLAUDE\WHEREDNGN\docs\strategy\_transcripts\JUT6N-eZwD8_09_most_essential_tahreeb.ar-orig.srt`
**Title:** اسهل اشكال في التهريب (the most essential / easiest forms of Tahreeb)
**Scope:** Three Tahreeb forms — same-suit opposite-shape, opposite-color, opposite-shape from-the-floor (twice).
**Disclaimer:** Auto-generated SRT — Arabic transcription contains diacritic and word-break errors (e.g. `هاس`/`خاس`/`خاص` for "Hearts"; `الشريعه`/`الشريحه` for "Spades"; `دائما`/`دايمن`/`الدايم` for "Diamonds"; `جيمر` is likely `ديمن`). Quoted text is preserved verbatim.

---

## Q1. 70/25/5 receiver prior (suit preference distribution)

**Verbatim Arabic (≤15w):** «بنسبه 70%… ممكن نقول 25%… نقول خمسه في المئه»
**English:** "at 70%… we could say 25%… we say five percent" — assumed prior over partner's wanted suit when partner tahreebs once. Highest-probability target ~70%, second-most-likely ~25%, lowest ~5%.
**Timestamp:** 00:01:22 → 00:01:34 (cues 71, 73, 77)
**Context:** Partner tahreebed Diamonds first. We assume he wants Hearts at 70%; could want Spades but at lower 25%; could want Diamonds (always-trump) at very low 5%.
**Confidence:** HIGH — three percentages stated explicitly in the same passage and referenced as the per-shape prior.

---

## Q2. Confidence ramp-up — second same-suit Tahreeb → ~90%

**Verbatim Arabic (≤15w):** «حتفترض بنسبه 90% انه يبغى خاص»
**English:** "you'll assume at 90% he wants Hearts" — after a second confirming tahreeb (small→big within the same suit), the prior on the wanted suit jumps from 70% to 90%.
**Timestamp:** 00:03:05 → 00:03:09 (cues 145, 147)
**Context:** Partner first tahreebed Jack of Diamonds, then Nine of Diamonds (big→small in second example) — speaker switches to Hearts-wanting at 90%. Reads as the post-second-tahreeb posterior.
**Confidence:** HIGH — explicit numerical claim in clear context.

---

## Q3. Two-time small-to-big → 100% "no debate"

**Verbatim Arabic (≤15w):** «خلاص مئه في الميه ما يبغى دي»
**English:** "done — 100%, he doesn't want Diamonds" — when tahreeb appears twice and the second tahreeb is from-bigger-to-smaller (read as opposite confirmation pattern), partner's non-want of the led suit is certain.
**Timestamp:** 00:03:00 → 00:03:02 (cues 141, 142)
**Context:** Speaker walks through "in the beginning he tahreebed Jack, then Nine — from big to small / from small to big" and concludes 100% certainty about Diamonds being unwanted.
**Confidence:** HIGH — verbatim "100%" stated in plain Arabic.

---

## Q4. "Biggest mistake" — never play absolute lowest after Tahreeb-led trick

**Verbatim Arabic (≤15w):** «هذا اكبر غلط في البلد»
**English:** "this is the biggest mistake in Baloot" — said about playing the lowest card (the seven, in the example) when sending a Tahreeb signal, which signals nothing because partner cannot tell you have a higher card you're protecting.
**Timestamp:** 00:06:05 → 00:06:08 (cues 271, 272)
**Context:** Sender side. You hold strong Spades (e.g. K-7, with 10 elsewhere) and want partner to lead Spades. The mistake is leading low (7) — you should keep your strength and play the 10 to be tahreebed, then on second pass tahreeb a higher card that confirms the want-message.
**Confidence:** HIGH — direct verbatim quote with full surrounding rationale.

---

## Q5. Direction encoding — small→big = WANT; big→small = DON'T WANT

**Verbatim Arabic (≤15w):** «تهريب يصغر لكبير او من كبير لصغير»
**English:** "tahreeb [going] from-small-to-big or from-big-to-small" — the speaker explicitly contrasts the two directional patterns and ties them to opposite meanings within the same example.
**Timestamp:** 00:02:56 → 00:02:58 (cues 137, 138)
**Companion verbatim (Q5b, dir → meaning chain):** «هرب لي سبيت اذا يا يبغى هاس» — "he tahreebed me Spades, so he wants Hearts" (00:01:12, cue 63), and «من تحت لفوق» = "from-bottom-to-top" (00:00:53 cue 47, and 00:02:45 cue 129) used as the WANT direction marker.
**English (Q5b):** Bottom-to-top (small→big) over two tahreebs = WANT the off-color/cross-shape; top-to-bottom (big→small) = the inverse (do not want, or want the led suit's complement). The example at 00:02:45 explicitly says "he tahreebed Jack first, then King — wants Diamond." The example at 00:02:58 inverts it: "Jack then Nine (big→small)" = 100% does NOT want Diamonds.
**Confidence:** HIGH (direction terminology); MEDIUM-HIGH on the strict semantic mapping — speaker uses both pairings but the encoding is implicit rather than stated as a single rule sentence.

---

## Q6. Tahreeb 5 forms enumerated (Tanfeer ⊃ Tahreeb ⊃ Bargiya, etc.)

**Verbatim Arabic (≤15w):** «تهريب نفس اللون عكس الشكل تهريب عكس اللون تهريب عكس شكل الارض»
**English:** "tahreeb same-color opposite-shape; tahreeb opposite-color; tahreeb opposite-shape of-the-floor" — three of the forms named directly. The video states it covers "the three remaining forms" of tahreeb (cue 5).
**Timestamp:** 00:00:07 → 00:00:11 (cues 5, 7, 9)
**Context:** Video #09's scope is the THREE remaining tahreeb forms (the easier ones), explicitly referencing that the first video in the series covered the others (cue 11: «وهذه موجوده في اول مقطع في التهريب»). Speaker also notes the from-the-floor form requires happening "twice" (cue 9: «ويكون من مرتين»).
**The five-form taxonomy (Tanfeer ⊃ Tahreeb ⊃ Bargiya hierarchy):** **NOT enumerated as a hierarchy in this video.** Video #09 names only three forms by name and references the prior video for the rest. The Bargiya / Tanfeer parent-relationship is alluded to only obliquely via «الاكه البرقيه» (the AKA-Bargiya, cue 45) being equated with single-card tahreeb-from-bottom-to-top.
**Confidence:** MEDIUM on the three forms (verbatim); LOW on the full 5-form Tanfeer⊃Tahreeb⊃Bargiya hierarchy — that hierarchy is **not** explicitly stated in this transcript and would need to come from video #08 or a glossary cross-reference.

## Q7. Section 4 rule 1B — second-lowest re-entry signal

**Verbatim Arabic (≤15w):** «اذا عندك قوه الافضل انك تحتفظ فيها وتخليها للاخير»
**English:** "if you have strength, it's better to keep it and leave it for last" — the receiver-side / sender-side guidance to retain the high card and play the second-best (the 10 in the example, holding back the K-7 trump structure or equivalent). This is the kernel of "second-lowest re-entry."
**Timestamp:** 00:05:45 → 00:05:51 (cues 255, 257)
**Companion verbatim (Q7b):** «العشره لمن تهربها غير لما تهرب البنت» — "the Ten when you tahreeb is different from tahreebing the Jack" (00:06:24, cues 285-287). The 10 carries a stronger want-message than the Jack because it shows you're sacrificing the largest non-honor in the led suit.
**Context:** The sender's second-pass card choice. Don't lead absolute-low (Q4 mistake); don't dump your honor; play the second-rung card (10) as the tahreeb body so partner reads "I gave up my biggest non-honor — I really want this."
**Confidence:** HIGH for the keep-strength-for-last principle; MEDIUM on labeling it "second-lowest re-entry" — the transcript's wording is closer to "second-highest you can spare" / "the 10 not the Jack."

---

## Q8. Hand-shape preconditions for Tahreeb (محشور / packed-hand)

**Verbatim Arabic (≤15w):** **NOT FOUND** — the word محشور does not appear in this transcript.
**Closest in-transcript signal:** «انت قاطع في الهاس» = "you are cut [void] in Hearts" (00:05:23, cue 237) — used as the precondition that justifies a particular tahreeb direction (you're void, so you cannot want Hearts; therefore your tahreeb must mean you want the cross-suit/cross-color).
**English:** Video #09 frames the precondition negatively (void in some suit ⟹ cannot want it ⟹ your signal disambiguates) rather than positively (must be packed/محشور). The packed-hand framing is from a different video (likely #14 — see A-Src-05).
**Timestamp:** 00:05:23 → 00:05:29 (cues 237, 239)
**Confidence:** HIGH that محشور is absent here; HIGH on the void-based precondition framing.

---

## Q9. Sender ledger semantics — when does sender's tahreebSent fire vs not?

**Verbatim Arabic (≤15w):** «لازم تاكل مره رابعه» — "you must eat the fourth time"
**English:** The sender's tahreeb is only complete when the trick-eat sequence reaches a fourth confirming round; before that, partner is still resolving the ambiguity. Specifically, sender ledger should fire on the second cross-suit dump (the confirming tahreeb), not on the first.
**Timestamp:** 00:04:32 → 00:04:35 (cues 197, 199)
**Companion verbatim (Q9b — first-pass disambiguation):** «تهريب يحتاج تهريب مره ثانيه» = "tahreeb needs a second tahreeb [to confirm]" (00:00:53, cue 49). And «انت مشيت على اصول التهريب عشان توضح اكثر» = "you walked the tahreeb's principles to clarify more" (00:04:30, cue 197).
**Operational rule:** sender's `tahreebSent` flag should fire when:
1. The sender plays a non-led-suit card (cross-suit dump) with intent to signal — first time = ambiguous (could be plain void); second time same-or-related-suit + directional ordering = **confirmed tahreeb signal**, ledger fires.
2. The sender plays the absolute-lowest of the dumped suit → does NOT fire as a meaningful signal (Q4 — "biggest mistake," reads as no-signal).
3. If sender has run out of follow-suit and is forced to dump, the speaker explicitly says (cues 109, 111) the receiver should still account for whether the sender "had nothing to eat with" — i.e. forced dumps should reduce signal-confidence, not fire as full tahreeb.
**Confidence:** MEDIUM-HIGH on the "needs second tahreeb to confirm" semantics; MEDIUM on the absolute-lowest exclusion; LOW-MEDIUM on the forced-dump exception (transcript hints at it but does not formalize).

---

## Q10. Partner-winning precondition strictness

**Verbatim Arabic (≤15w):** «خويك اللي ياكل وانت اللي تهرب لخويك»
**English:** "your partner is the one who eats / wins the trick, and you are the one who tahreebs to your partner" — the strict precondition. Tahreeb is a signal sent ONLY when partner is winning the trick at the moment you play your card.
**Timestamp:** 00:04:50 → 00:04:52 (cues 211, 213)
**Companion verbatim:** «تبغى توصل له رساله» = "you want to deliver him a message" (00:04:52, cue 213) — frames tahreeb as exclusively a partner-directed message, not a defensive discard.
**Operational rule:** Partner-winning is a HARD precondition for tahreeb interpretation. If opponent is winning the trick and you dump, that is NOT tahreeb (could be a forced void, a defensive low, or a cut decision). Same for if partner has not yet played — speaker's example chain always assumes partner has eaten / will eat the trick before sender's tahreeb card lands.
**Confidence:** HIGH — explicit role-reversal sentence anchors the precondition. The sender/receiver framework presumed throughout video #09 is partner-wins-trick.

---

## Cross-reference notes (for downstream consumers)

- **Video #09 vs #14 split:** packed-hand (محشور) precondition is in #14 (see A-Src-05); void-cut precondition is in #09 (this doc). Both must be treated as alternative — not equivalent — preconditions.
- **Direction-encoding (Q5)** is the operational core. Implementations must track tahreeb-pair ordering (small→big vs big→small) per suit, not just "tahreeb count."
- **70/25/5 prior (Q1)** can be used as the initial Bayesian seed for `S.s.tahreebSuitPosterior` (or equivalent state field). Update to (90/?/?) on second confirming tahreeb (Q2).
- **Sender ledger (Q9)** fires on confirmed (=2nd) tahreeb, not on first ambiguous dump. Avoid double-firing. The "biggest mistake" guard (Q4) means absolute-lowest dumps should be filtered OUT of tahreeb candidates entirely.
- **Partner-winning gate (Q10)** is a hard predicate. If `currentTrickWinner ≠ partnerSeat` at sender's play, set `tahreebSent = false` regardless of card choice.

---

**Extractor confidence overall:** HIGH on Q1, Q2, Q3, Q4, Q5, Q10. MEDIUM on Q7, Q9. LOW-MEDIUM on Q6 (5-form hierarchy not in this video). HIGH-NEGATIVE on Q8 (محشور absent — record this as a finding, not a gap).
